text.title: Engagement Levels and Transition Strategies
text.summary: Five engagement levels with observable indicators and concrete tactics for moving a stakeholder one level at a time.

section.title: Engagement Level Definitions
section.p: Each level is defined by observable behaviour, not assumed attitude.

list.item: Unaware — no contact, no information received about the project
Resistant — actively opposes; blocks decisions, escalates concerns
Neutral — aware but indifferent; neither helps nor hinders
Supportive — advocates internally; provides resources or air cover
Leading — drives adoption; removes barriers on behalf of the project

rule.statement: Classify stakeholder level from behavioural evidence only (attendance, response time, public statements, decisions made). Never classify from assumed intent.
rule.rationale: Polite emails read as Neutral by automated tools and optimistic PMs; actual engagement level is revealed by action, not tone.

rule.statement: Move stakeholders one level at a time. Never plan a jump from Resistant directly to Leading — earn each step with a concrete commitment.
rule.rationale: Skipping levels produces shallow engagement that collapses under the first pressure. Resistant stakeholders who feel manipulated regress further.

example.note: Resistant to Neutral — IT Security Director blocking a cloud migration.
example.code: Strategy: address concerns directly, involve in requirements definition. Activities: Week 1: 1:1 meeting — listen, do not defend; document their specific concerns. Week 2: Security requirements workshop — they co-author the requirements. Week 3: Vendor security certification review session. Week 4: Joint sign-off on security acceptance criteria. Evidence of success: written confirmation of participation in Week 4 sign-off.

example.note: Neutral to Supportive — Operations Manager on an efficiency project.
example.code: Strategy: demonstrate personal benefit; ask for their input. Activities: - Invite to pilot design session (their domain). - Quantify efficiency gains in their team's KPIs, not generic metrics. - Ask for their endorsement on the pilot scope document. Evidence of success: Manager signs pilot scope document or publicly references the benefit.

example.note: Supportive to Leading — Sales Director at product launch.
example.code: Strategy: give visible role; share strategic information; public recognition. Activities: - Assign speaking role at launch event. - Include in weekly strategy sessions (information access signals trust). - Feature in internal success story communication. Evidence of success: Director proactively recruits other supporters without being asked.

list.item: Declining meeting attendance — disengaging; trigger direct outreach within 48 hours
Sending a delegate — reducing personal priority; understand the cause before escalating
Negative public comments — active resistance signal; require private conversation, not email
Going to executives — escalating concern; address the underlying issue immediately
Silent in meetings — withholding; follow up 1:1 before the next group session

antipattern.description: Treating all stakeholders the same — identical email cadence, identical information level.
antipattern.reason: Stakeholders at different levels and with different power need different content, channel, and frequency. One-size comms accelerates disengagement.

antipattern.description: Assuming that support earned once is permanent.
antipattern.reason: Engagement decays under competing priorities, project delays, or broken commitments. Leading stakeholders who feel ignored revert to Neutral within weeks.
